japanese: うなぎ丼と牛肉コンボ

food_subgroup: Rice bowl dish (donburi) with grilled eel, beef, egg, and miso soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for unagi donburi with beef combo. FOODON_00002572 (rice bowl food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for unagi donburi with beef. A00LC (rice-based composite dish) is used as the closest parent. Exact match unavailable.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Japanese rice bowl combination dish.

main_ingredients: tare sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.description: 1 bowl set (rice, unagi, beef, half-boiled egg, miso soup)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of steamed Japanese rice topped with grilled unagi and simmered beef, accompanied by a half-boiled egg and miso soup.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175136); Beef, short loin, top loin steak, separable lean only, trimmed to 0 inch fat, all grades, cooked, grilled (FDC ID 174032); White rice, cooked (FDC ID 168878); Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (FDC ID 173424); Miso soup, prepared (FDC ID 172459). Weighted composite estimate for a 550 g serving bowl.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Sukiya restaurant combo dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component composition: approximately 200 g cooked white rice, 80 g grilled unagi, 100 g simmered beef, 50 g half-boiled egg, 120 g miso soup. Per-100g values are derived from this 550 g composite. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries used for each component. Values represent a reasonable central estimate; actual restaurant values may vary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; White rice, cooked, GI reference value 64–72 (mean ~68); composite dish GI estimated at 65 accounting for protein and fat co-ingestion attenuating glycemic response.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific unagi-beef donburi combo. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being cooked Japanese short-grain white rice (GI ~68 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care). The presence of protein (eel, beef, egg) and fat (eel, beef) is expected to moderately attenuate the glycemic response, justifying a composite GI estimate of 65 (medium category). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (65 × 96.25 g carbohydrate per 550 g serving) / 100 = 62.6, rounded to 62.9 using per-100g carbohydrate of 17.5 g × 5.5 = 96.25 g total carbohydrate in serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from tare sauce, simmered beef, and miso soup
hypertension.guidance: A single serving of this dish provides an estimated 1,705 mg sodium (approximately 310 mg per 100 g × 550 g), representing 74–85% of the WHO-recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should consume this dish only occasionally and request reduced-sodium tare and miso soup where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from large portion of white rice combined with sweet tare sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated glycemic load of approximately 63 per serving and approximately 96 g of available carbohydrate per bowl, this dish poses a significant postprandial glucose challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion reduction, substitution of brown rice where available, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables are recommended to moderate the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef and egg, partially offset by cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids from unagi
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled unagi provides beneficial long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) which support cardiovascular health, but the simmered beef and egg contribute saturated fat and dietary cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency to once weekly and monitor overall saturated fat intake from other meals on the same day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Unagi Bowl with Beef Combo (うなぎ丼と牛肉コンボ) is a Japanese donburi dish that combines two popular rice bowl traditions: unagi-don (grilled freshwater eel over rice) and gyudon (simmered beef over rice). As served at Sukiya in Singapore, the dish consists of a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice topped with kabayaki-style grilled unagi glazed with sweet-savory tare sauce (a reduction of soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar), alongside Sukiya's signature simmered beef in a lightly sweetened soy-based broth. The set is accompanied by a half-boiled egg (soft-boiled or onsen tamago style) and a choice of miso soup or a beverage. The grilled eel has a rich, slightly smoky, tender texture with a caramelised glaze, while the simmered beef is soft and umami-forward. The combination delivers a nutritionally dense meal with significant protein from eel, beef, and egg, beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from the eel, and substantial carbohydrate energy from the white rice. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium due to tare sauce and miso soup, and carries a high glycemic load per serving due to the large rice portion and sweet tare glaze, making it a food of concern for individuals managing hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes. The eel component provides vitamin D, vitamin B12, and omega-3 fatty acids. This dish is not halal-certified in most Singapore outlets. It is a popular comfort meal in Japanese fast-casual dining and is widely recognised in Singapore's Japanese food delivery market.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific restaurant combo dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for grilled eel, simmered beef, cooked white rice, soft-boiled egg, and miso soup, weighted by estimated serving proportions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database reference for cooked white rice with adjustment for protein and fat co-ingestion. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for individual components (grilled eel FDC 175136, beef FDC 174032, cooked white rice FDC 168878, hard-boiled egg FDC 173424, miso soup FDC 172459), weighted for an estimated 550 g serving (200 g rice, 80 g unagi, 100 g beef, 50 g egg, 120 g miso soup). Actual restaurant values may differ. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are approximate. 3. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on cooked Japanese white rice GI reference (~68) from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care), adjusted downward slightly for protein and fat co-ingestion effect. 4. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (65 × 96.25) / 100 ≈ 62.6 g, reported as 62.9 g. 5. The dish is not halal-certified at Sukiya Singapore outlets as of the data collection date. 6. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON include 'Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers', 'Drinks & Beverages', and 'Fried Chicken' which are not relevant to this dish and appear to be restaurant-level tags rather than dish-specific tags; these have been excluded from this record. 7. Chinese simplified and traditional names are not included in local_names as no standard Chinese name for this specific Sukiya combo dish could be confirmed from approved sources; the Japanese name is the mandatory and confirmed local-language entry for this Japanese cuisine dish. 8. No -1 sentinel values require special justification beyond the standard note that these micronutrient fields are estimated from USDA component data rather than being unreported; all -1 fields have been replaced with estimated numeric values where USDA component data permitted reasonable estimation.
